online_test.description:
Round 1(Online Test, 107 mins): This online test was taken on Oracle's test platform and was divided into various sections.
•    There were only MCQ-type questions, with no negative marking. Once you start a section you have to attempt it entirely, as you cannot come back to it. Once you click submit for a particular question as well, you cannot come back to it.
•    Every section was individually timed, and there was a section-wise cut-off for marks as well for clearing this round.
questions: •Section 1, consist of English communication questions, there were comprehension-based questions only.

•In section 2, there were various data structures questions about binary trees and AVL trees(actually all the questions were of binary tree and AVL trees, although paper was in set all the student were having different questions).

•In the mental ability section(Section 3), there were various sub-sections for logical ability, data analysis and critical thinking, attention to detail, and quantitative ability. Keep track of time, as time was harder to manage.
•    In the CS fundamentals section, there were questions on DBMS, OOPs, Computer Networks and Operating Systems. These were of medium difficulty.

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description:
Round 2(Technical Interview, 50 mins): The round started on a very friendly note, the interviewer was really nice and made some small talk with me, asking how my family is doing, and how I've been keeping busy and managing time.
•    Then we moved on to resume discussion, I was asked about one project on my resume (project on DBMS). He asked about my role in the project and various doubts that he had about the project.

•    Then we did move to discuss about my past work experience which includes a freelance in web dev and sales and marketing intern which I did during pandemic.

•    He also did ask about my extra-curricular, achievements and Position of responsibility (web developer at TEDxNITW).

•    Once this resume discussion was over, he moved on to a technical question to be solved on a shared notepad, which was an easy one as we had less time left after our resume discussion.

•    Question was to implement a linked list with proper object-oriented programming and create stack and queues using that linked list class as parent classes and stack and queue as child classes.

•    After this round I was asked to wait in the waiting room for 20-30 mins before the next round begin.

interview_rounds.title: Round 3
interview_rounds.description: Round 3(Technical Interview, 65 mins): This round focused less on my resume, but much more on logic, thinking ability, and DBMS. He did asked me logical and theoretical questions about DBMS as the interviewer was very friendly and supportive so he keep giving me hints I was unable to give the expected answer, after this he shared a link of some online platform where I was asked to write 10-12 SQL queries, after which interviewer seems to be satisfied, he also did asked me the practical implementation of C++ which I was unaware off, so he said that knowing the practical/real life implementation of technologies helps us or motivates us to learn them and leads to a proper growth in life. After this as we were left with very less time so just to check if I am able to write any programming language, he did ask me to write a code of Fibonacci number and after that I was asked to wait for 1 hour before my HR round begins (includes a half an hour of lunch break).

interview_rounds.title: Round 4
interview_rounds.description:
Round 4(HR Interview, 40 mins): This was a small and easy round, it started with some questions about my internship experience, they wanted to know more about my role and the technology stack that I had worked on.
Also, the challenges I have faced in life and about the POR's in my resume, he also asked about the problems we faced while working in a team and some more hr questions like my biggest failure and biggest success.
Which went well and we have to wait for 5 hours after that for the final list.

other_comments:
Final comments
1. Keep calm and don't be nervous. The interviewers are very friendly.
2. In the case of Oracle, you must be well prepared with DBMS, OOP, Java and Data Structures. A lot of fellow interviewees were asked questions on all these. DBMS is especially important.
3. Last and the most important, it is of cardinal importance that you do all questions that KR sir has taught in class, revise his notes and practice coding regularly. I could solve the AVL Tree question in the Online round only because I remembered sir's notes.
4. For DBMS, watch the videos of Sanchit Jain on YouTube and revise class notes. Don't forget to practice SQL.
5. Keep practicing questions in Java and learn all its concepts properly
6. Learn OOP concepts really well. The interviewers will try to pick confusing scenarios to test you on that.

online_test.description: Be prepared about basic arrays, linked lists, strings and matrix questions. Be good in maps and sets especially. They are super useful.
questions: Basic string/array/matrix problem.

A better problem revolving on the same. Or sometimes basic graph or tree questions you can expect. Like floor/ceil/inorder successor/vertical traversal etc for trees and for graphs also basic BFS and DFS.
Yes MCQs will be there. Lots of logical reasoning, english grammar and some little business concepts like stats related to profit, loss, etc.

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description: Round 1 and 2 can be interchanged. But basically one round consists of totally project based and little oops, dbms, (depending on your project), and then a little math or super easy coding question to top it up. Can last for total 1 hour. It depends, I mean totally on how much rapport you build with them. For me the ma'am was really impressed by my project work and I explained it also from financial pt. of view what benefits the projects can do. They test your versatility and can be impressed. But then if you do only one project on one topic it gets weird for the interviewer. So cover multiple areas.

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description: Again, as I said rounds 1 and 2 could be interchanged. Mostly DS, auite logical but hard level. Need to think. Not necessary that it would be a complicated data structure. It could also be a hard level problem of leetcode, for basic stuff like lists, arrays or strings. Also, a lot of analysis on time complexity. Also they may start or end with a project question. Just to make the interview a little abstract.

interview_rounds.title: Round 3
interview_rounds.description: HR. Simple and sweet.

other_comments: Be good at basics. Be versatile. Talk business. I mean, be friendly and crisp, to the point. Make sure you stress on the financial applications of the concepts you talk about. Like suppose blockchain. What benefits it could do to that company, or what is that company's role in it? etc. etc. It's wise to learn up a little on that. Other than that its cool.

online_test.description:
Amazon OT was conducted on AMCET platform, there were sections consisting of code debugging that was pretty much easy, aptitude questions- medium level, not so difficult, coding section- two coding questions were there and you need to complete the given function, another section was behavioural section which consist lot of questions based on behaviour and leadership principles and the last was feedback section, without submitting feedback your test is not considered  for evaluation.

Section wise points-
Code debug- the questions are pretty much easier, you need not to prepare for this, you can complete each question in less than one minute, just read the question properly in one go only
Aptitude section- basic maths till class 10th level, look out for some questions one or two days before ot, indiabix is a good option for this level of questions
Coding round- Although no one can say about pattern, but amazon if fond of trees, it will only ask standard question may it be from any topic
Behaviour round- There are a lot of questions, as soon you progress you'll notice that they are related to the previous ones. Amazon values it's leadership principles the most, go through each and every point before appearing for test, they will help them for solving these questions, the questions are like will be going in a way depending on how you answered your previous question, always keep in mind what you answered for previous one, same questions may repeat, stick only on one approach.

was related to maximum average  of subtree with little bit of modification.

this was also tree based question, a variation of subtree problem we were required to perform certain operations given.

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description:
For amazon there was only one round. My round started with general discussion like where i am, everything good or not and then introduction, the interviewer introduced himself, asked me to do the same, i was doing but after telling name and branch he stopped me. The interviewer was a very senior person with a very good position. He shared me the link for the editor where i wrote my code and both can see that.
He explained me the question, the question was pretty much confusing at first, then he gave me some examples which helped me to understand what the question is. My way of solving the question was i divided the question n certain steps and started solving step by step, i told him my approach for each and every step and if he found that suitable he told me to code that. After a long discussion i gave him my complete solution, he then said me to optimise my code, within two to three minutes i did that, he further said me to optimize it more, for out of bound inputs, i struggled with that, came up to a solution but that was still less optimized as per him. First question discussion took 35 mins for us. Then he moved to next question which was a very good modification of buying and selling of stocks, he gave me six parameters, and a formula, and said that i am not interested in profit or loss, at any point of time i want to calculate this formula so work  with this only, i struggled with this, was stuck at a point and told him that i'm not able to get an approach, then he gave me a hint and using that with certain discussion i gave him my solution, he further said me for some modifications, i did that and at the end he was completely satisfied with my solution, he told me that this is what i was asking

then he asked me that do you have any other interview, i denied and he ended the meet.

Important points that i found useful-
-If you are stuck, don't show this on your face, be calm and keep trying, don't be quite also, you'll surely come up with the solution.
-be confident, always keep a little smile up, keep interacting with the interviewer
- be confident about what you said. In my case whenever i told him that sir i'll do this, everytime he said me to code and then explain.

online_test.description: OT consists of 2 DSA questions to be solved in 1 hr. The questions are not the same for all. And the difficulties too are different. For me, the questions are of easy-medium difficulty.
questions:
Given a string consisting of alphanumeric characters. Now, we need to find the maximum substring which is a common prefix of the given string and the strings generated by removing any character from the given string.
Eg. Given string s="abcder"
The strings formed after removing a single character are : bcder, acder, abder, abcer, abcdr, abcde

This is an easy question. Simple brute force is accepted. I think that's the only possible way to solve this.

An array of weights of stones is given. The worker has to break them into pieces. He always takes the 2 most weighted stones and breaks them. If both are of equal weight, they will be completely broken. Or else the smaller one will break completely but the larger one is reduced by the weight of the smaller stone. Finally, he will be left with either one stone or none. What will be the weight of the stone if left?

This is an easy-medium question according to the opinion of some other students. I solved this using a priority queue.

interview_rounds.title: round 2
interview_rounds.description:
18 students got shortlisted for the interview. I was pretty sure that I will be shortlisted for interviews because the OT went very well.
For internships, there is only one round. It is a technical round.

Started with my introduction for around 2 minutes. Then he asked me to give a brief about my project. I along with my friend did an android app as a project.

(PROMOTION😅: The app is called "CPing". This will be very much useful for competitive programmers and for those who are interested in participating in hackathons. MAKE SURE TO CHECK THIS OUT IN THE PLAYSTORE😉)

After giving a brief about the project, he started digging deeper into it.
I told him that I don't have any idea about the backend and API. And it is done by the other guy.
1) Draw a flow chart kind of thing that describes the complete structure of the project.
2) In the diagram, a small part is regarding the API and extracting the details from the web. He m̶o̶s̶t̶l̶y̶   only focussed on that part🤷‍♀️. He asked many questions about that part for half an hour.
3) And asked a few questions about the database I used in the project.

This process took around 45 minutes. Then he asked about what are the technical skills I am planning to learn in the coming days. I told him that I did a little bit of ML, and I want to explore more about that. He asked me few basic questions regarding ML. (If you really did something regarding ML, you can definitely answer these). This part of the interview is good because I answered all the questions.😂

At last, Do you have any questions?
Use this chance properly. Ask proper questions that make him feel that you are very much interested in joining that company.

SAD PART OF THE INTERVIEW:  No DSA, No OOPS, No DBMS🤷‍♂️

other_comments:
Results came after 5 hrs. I am pretty sure that I'm not gonna select and preparing for another OT. But, still, there is hope. Because:
1) I never maintained silence during the interview.
2) I was continuously asking him questions and clarifying whether I am in the right way or not.
3) I had a very good idea about my project and I showed him this while giving a brief about it.

And yeah, I was selected.

I rejected 10 rejections by accepting this intern offer from VISA.

SUGGESTIONS:
1) NEVER EVER NEGLECT CGPA(like me; I had 7.57 which is very less😒), IT IS VERY IMPORTANT AT EVERY STAGE OF THE PROCESS. UNTIL & UNLESS YOU ARE NOT INTERESTED IN JOINING ANY COMPANY, & NOT GOING FOR HIGHER STUDIES. Sometimes solving all the questions in OT doesn't give you a chance for interviews, only because of CGPA. So, take care of it.
2) Have a good idea about the project.
3) Make sure you also know what your team members did and how you used it.

That's it!
ALL THE VERY BEST.

other_comments:
The ot was pretty easy.Little optimised brute force worked for given questions.
The technical interviews were also pretty simple compared to other companies.
In my case , dbms and oops were not asked ,tech rounds were purely based on dsa,coding.

Since, this was my very first interview experience, i did some minor mistakes while giving interviews.

1. Without explaining brute force , i directly went to optimised solution ,because i solved some problems earlier.For any interview , start with basic and go on improvising it. Because , the interviewer will judge your thought process and understanding of concepts ,he doesn't just want the correct answer.

2. Try your best until, very last moment, don't give up  ,take the hints given by interviewer.
In my case, in round-2 , i solved 1,2 questions very quickly and when he improvised 2nd question i was stuck and wasn't able to solve ,then he gave me a hint,even then i wasn't able to solve. After interview, when i shared my experience ,my frnd who already solved that problem gave me same hint given by interviewer ,then i got the idea immediately🙂. So, stay calm during the interview, don't get nervous when you are stuck and give your best.

Only writing exams and giving interviews are in your hands , getting shortlisted is not in your hands.
So, give your best and whatever maybe the result just accept it.
Finally,your hard-work will definitely payoff one day,so just wait for your day.

ALL THE BEST!!!👍👍

online_test.description:
1 hour for 2 coding questions+ Mcqs.

questions: Start with a given array of integers and an arbitrary initial value x. Calculate the running sum of x plus each array element, from left to right. The running sum must never get below 1. Determine the minimum value of x.

You are a school student who is attending math class, and your teacher has asked everyone in class to bring a calculator so she could teach her class how to use one. The teacher decides that the first lesson would be, where she would tell a positive number and the students just have to type the exact number into the calculator.
But, just as the class is about to begin you find that some of the numbers on the calculator are broken totally while some are working, and only the "multiplication" and "equal to" buttons are working. Since you are a smart kid, you decide that you still want to get to the number that the teacher has just told out loud, as quickly as possible with what you have.
Example, if your teacher said the number 60, and you can type 1, 2, 5 only. You will push the buttons:
15 (2 clicks)
multiplication (1 click)
2 (1 click)
multiplication (1 click)
2 (1 click)
equal to (1 click)
which requires 7 clicks, but since you are smart you use: 12 (2 clicks), multiplication (1 click), 5 (1 click), equal to (1 click) which is just 5 clicks in all. You want to get to the number as fast as possible, so you have to minimize the number of clicks.

online_test.description:
This is regarding my Uber She++ 2021 program experience. We had to give an online coding test for the program after which 75 top girl candidates were selected from engineering colleges across India. I was among the selected candidates along with two others from our campus. We all were invited for a two day online networking event. It was an amazing experience with lots of fun activities, interactive sessions and talks from the engineers about Uber's work culture, values, diversity and opportunities available. The women leaders of the office shared their inspiring experiences and knowledge. We had interactions with the recent interns where they shared OT and interview tips, resources and other guidance.
A few days after the event we had a second OT for immediate summer internship. Later, for our on campus intern drive, all three of us were given direct interview without needing to attend OT.
Both the tests were conducted on CodeSignal Platform. The duration was 1 hour and we had to complete three coding questions. There was no proctoring. The topics mainly focused on were Dynamic programming and graphs.
questions: The first question was pretty simple. We had to find the largest plus sign made up of 1s in a binary matrix.
The second was to find all permutations of a given string that are palindromes.

Third was a query-based problem on arrays which I solved using pointers.
Given dimensions of a rectangular board m and n where the rows and columns are numbered and each cell's value is the product of its indices i*j. Initially all cells are activated and we need to process 3 kinds of queries, type 1 deactivates all cells of a particular row, type 2 deactivates all cells of a particular column and third one we need to print the active cell having minimum value among all that remain.
Approach: Setting two variables both at 0 0 initially, just had two arrays one storing rows 1 to m and other storing columns 1 to n. Whenever any is deactivated it is changed to -1 in the array, when the print query is called, the pointer is just shifted to the next minimum active position.

Those that were able to clear all three questions with all test cases passing within the given time were selected. There was partial marking for the number of test cases passed. You can navigate and switch between the questions as many times as you want and can submit multiple times.

We have to call two students at a time, multiply their values and add it to cost, send one back home(remove from array) and other back to their seat. Repeat with remaining students until only one remains finally. We had to do it for either maximum or minimum cost, i don't remember exactly.

online_test.description: I was selected for Internship through the Microsoft Engage program. It was a month long mentorship program at the end of which those candidates that performed satisfactorily were offered direct interview for internship, and a few like me got offered direct internship. While registering we had to submit our resume and other details ike CGPA which also had points in the final decision. The initial selection for the program was done through an Online Test. It was 45 minutes long and consisted of 15 questions on both algorithms and coding. It mainly focused on the topics of DSA and DAA.
questions: My first coding question was to implement priority queues in any language of my choice(full code, no STL) using arrays. We had to implement the structure, and functions for inserting an element, deleting an element, extracting minimum and extracting the maximum element. Can be implemented in C++ using heap structure.

The second was a problem based on optimal stopping. Among n possible choices, we had to maximize the probability of choosing the best value, and as we traverse the candidates, we have to either select the current as final choice and stop or reject them and move forward. According to a optimal strategy law, that sample size to be taken is 1/e of total.

Then, there were several descriptive questions based on algorithms and time complexities. We had to compare and contrast between different sorting algorithms, in terms of time and space complexities, best case and worst case scenarios and different inputs and choose the most efficient ones.

Rest of the questions were a mix of MCQs and descriptive type on algorithms, complexities, predicting output, and other similar concepts. One question that stood out was to explain recursion in simple, non-technical terms, as in, how you would describe the concept and its working to your grandmother.
Overall, the key for this OT was speed and completing as many questions correctly as possible within the time constraints.

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description:
The Project:
Since I didn't have an interview, I shall explain my experience of the program. This event's challenge was to build a clone of Microsoft Teams within the span of a month. We had to fulfil a minimum requirement that at least two users can connect through a video call. We could use any tech stack of our choice and build either a web application or mobile application. We were divided into groups of 6-7 participants and were assigned two mentors who were Microsoft engineers. The month was divided into several sprints, each a week long and we had to complete few milestones at the end of each sprint and update our mentors about it. My mentors were super friendly and supportive. At the end of the process we had to submit our project with a presentation and demo video for evaluation.
I made my project, Engage Meets using NodeJS backend and React frontend. The video calling facilities were implemented using WebRTC and Socket.io. Its features include real-time call using meet id, in-call chatting, screen sharing, mic, camera and screen controls and social media sharing.

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description:
The Program:
Throughout the program we had several insightful sessions, about Microsoft and their work culture and the different opportunities they offer. We had talks with previous year interns and Microsoft engineers, who shared their experiences and tips. The theme of the program was based on Agile Methodology, which was a Project Management technique followed at Microsoft and they expected us to do the same for our project. We had few workshops on the same and had to do a few assignments on the aspects and concepts of Agile. The quizzes were purely MCQs and they also contributed to our final scores and selections. In addition, we had a few workshops on Azure Communication Services as well.

interview_rounds.title: Round 3
interview_rounds.description:
Overall Experience:
The overall experience was amazing and very insightful. I was somewhat new to development at the time, thus the opportunity motivated me to learn and upskill myself in a short span. The mentors were always available for resolving any issues or queries and provided regular feedback and tips. Finally, all the factors that weighed in for the internship selections were the project evaluation, the quizzes, the mentor's feedback on our interactions, CGPA and resume. A few days before the campus intern drive, I received the offer for direct internship at Microsoft, without needing to give any OT or interview.

other_comments: My final suggestions to all the juniors are to keep your CGPA as high as you can and to diversify your horizons in tech. Several companies shortlist based on cg and resume thus it is very advantageous to keep these two factors impressive. There are several events and opportunities that show up in the summer period and I recommend you all not to miss out on any and give a fair try for all such opportunities. They can be valuable additions to the resume while also providing experience and other benefits. There are several separate off campus hiring programs, especially for girls, so keep watching out for them and apply. All the best and I wish you all success in your endeavours.

online_test.description: OT was divided into 4 sections . Section 1 had 2 easy to moderate coding questions which I was able to do quickly. Section 2 was based on aptitude. This was one of the most difficult sections as it had almost JEE level questions with topics ranging from probability , complex numbers, conic sections and others. I guess I just did ok in this one. Third section was an advanced coding based section  . I dont particularly remember the question but I think it we had to use DP to solve it. I was able to do it partially (8/15 testcases )which was good enough to qualify. Final section had mcqs that revolved around DBMS and OOPS concepts. The OT did go very well but I made it to the shortlist for round 1 of interview. Also each section had a fixed time allotted and we were allowed to shift freely from one section to other.

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description: Round 1 went pretty smoothly for me. The interviewer was nice and soon after greeting each other we began the interview. It was a technical round and he asked me 3 coding questions in total. The first question was "check whether a parenthesis sequence is balanced or not"(standard application of stacks). I solved it within few minutes explaining my approach while coding. He gave me the next question which was that " Given a m*n chessboard with a knight placed at source_x, source_y . Find whether the knight can reach the destination coordinates and also the minimum steps to do so". Again a standard question that I solved using bfs. He asked me one last question which was " Given a binary array where each element denoted a bulb and if the bulb was on,  the value of element would be 1 otherwise 0. I could do only one operation which is turn on an off bulb and for every bulb on which I applied this operation the state of all bulbs to its right will be flipped. I had to tell the minimum no of operations needed to switch on all the bulbs. " The key was to flip bulbs from left to right and maintain a flip count to do it in O(n). I told him my approach and he said it was correct. After this he asked why wanted to do an internship at GS and then what were my favourite subjects . The round turned out to be a lot easier that anticipated and he looked satisfied with my performance.

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description: This round began yet again with a coding question where I was told to find running median of a stream of integers. After this he gave me question where given a array of strings I had to group all the anagrams together. I was able to do both efficiently then he asked me about my projects which I explained to him. He then gave me a sort of a system design question where he asked me to address a hypothetical situation where I have been given a twitter like application and I had to find a way to deliver tweets of a celebrity (who is having millions of followers) to all his followers efficiently. I had to tell what data structures I would use and how will I execute this process efficiently. I told him that I will map user_id to his list of followers and will deliver tweets based on priority of their interest in this celebrity and also try to do it in batches to reduce load on server. We discussed a lot of possibilities and I wasn't sure whether I was taking the right approach but I was communicating every idea I had and he kept guiding me. After this he asked whether I had any  questions and I asked about the work he did and things expected from an intern . The round ended after this and I wasn't sure of making it to next round after the discussion at the end.

interview_rounds.title: round 2
interview_rounds.description: After waiting for almost 1.5 hrs unaware of any result, I was finally called for 3rd and final round. We began with brief introduction about me and then he asked me about the previous rounds. I told him about the 5 coding questions and the discussion at the end of round 2 . To my horror he said lets continue with the 2nd round discussion and asked me to code in some way whatever ideas I shared . Also he added that he wont judge me on the basis of this question but he wanted to see my approach which was some relief. For the next 20 mins or so I tried to express my ideas through my code which I didn't have a good feeling about but I tried my best to communicate everything I was doing and the reason behind it. Finally after a mess , he told me that he wanted to show me how difficult it was to implement solutions in comparison to just saying "oh i could do this and use that ..." which I totally agreed on. He then went on to ask me two HR questions. The first question was "Suppose I had been given a project and the instructions on how to do it. But I had a different method in mind and knew that it was better, which method would I choose?? ". My answer in brief --- I said I will try to explain my manager my method and try to convince him to allow me to do the project using my method. He said mangers are generally angry and don't like to discuss thing then what? I said I will obey my manager to which he said " should you do that despite knowing your method is better??" . Basically I told him every approach and he kept countering me,  asking me to be practical and think something else. I would like to add that the conversation all this while was pretty healthy and he even laughed a few times while countering me .... He then proceeded to ask another situation based question where I did not like the project given to me by my manager (with no idea about the technologies needed to complete it) and if I wasn't able to complete my job would be at risk. What would I do in such a situation ?? . Yet again he kept countering my answers and after some time he asked me about my DBMS project and told me write a SQL query. The round ended after me asking a couple of questions from him and he left the meeting after wishing me all the best for my future. I had communicated well throughout but did not know what to make of this round. I even told my friends that I don't think I'll make it to the final list. The final list came after 4-5 hours and to my surprise I was selected!!

other_comments:
Try to have a strong grip over DSA especially the standard questions.
Keep revising OOPS and DBMS although you might not see their questions in some OTs and interviews.
Be confident and communicate well in interviews.
Do not be disheartened by rejections and keep working hard , you will make it.

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description:
Round one was the easiest.
The interviewer was also very lite and i told him it was my first interview ever so he said i would make it as simple as possible.He asked me to implement a deque.i started by doing array implementation but when i asked if it is a circular deque he said no.So later i thought a while and did it with linked list.

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description:
Round2 was mainly based on whatever was written on my resume.I wrote that i know dbms dsa and oops.
She asked which is your favourite subject i told dsa
then she asked which is your favourite data structure and why i told bst as searching become very easy and less time
Then she asked how do u think a shopping site like amazon is implemented using dbms and oops.
This was a thinking question and i thought for a while and told that they must have a database having list of all tables of products,prices,brand,and type of product.
Then she asked to explain more.
I told her we can make a table corrosponding to each type of a product like one for electricals ,one for home accesories and household things etc...
Then she asked if there will be redundancy and how will u manage that.
i told her about normalisation and how to decompose table to avoid redundancy.(Like if a same table contains product name,price company,company customare care like that than we can decomepose it into two one for product and other for company having corresponding detail of each).She asked how would u link these two tables.I said using primary and foreign keys.
so like that she asked many questions how would u give recommendations if we have precalculated matching factors(for all products with the product we have to recommend).I told using self balancing bst.
Then she asked which ds uses self balancing bst i told Set and Maps.
After that she saw c++ as my main coding language she asked me few numericals on pointers and references.(It was a tough ones for me but i did explained how ptrs work )
Then she asked me a puzzle  A spider is making his web.each day the web work gets double.if 100% work takes 15days how much would 25% work take  (ans is 13d).
Then she asked about virtual function in c++.I told the concept but my code was giving error.(I never did oops in c++ so did'nt even know there is nothing like extend keyword in c++ rather we use ':')

interview_rounds.title: Round 3
interview_rounds.description:
round3 was purely question based although i was expecting hr round typical questions (Which i did prepared pre interview).
He asked some basic questions about queue what it is .
Then he told me to implement a queue which stores a key value pair  whenever we enqueue if that key is already present we update the value else we just enque.Dequeue would be normal dequeue.
He said is there any problem with your approach.I told that the enqueue operation becomes O(n) time and that is not idle.He asked me how would u optimize it.After thinking a while i said we will implement the queue using a linkedlist.And for keeping track of key value pairs we will have a map<int,Node*> in the queue structure itself.So whenever a enqueue occurs we check in map for that key if it is there we take pointer to that node from map and update the value.
After that he asked how would u dequeue that.I was stuck a bit at this as i do not know how to delete from map but later on i suggested at dequeue we will make map[value] as a null ptr and update the enqueue frunction(if key is present and map[key]!=NULL then update value in enqueue). He was satisfied very well with the logic and implementation.Nothing to code so far.
After this he asked me a question given a encoding pattern a--1 b--2 ...... z--26 what would be decode of 126
I said there are multiple ans az,abf ..
So he asked me how to calculate all the number of decodes.
So this was a type of dp question.I give recursiv solution was and then memoize the same later on.He asked complexity for recursive and dp code (2^n,O(n)).
the basic logic for recursive code is for decoding we can decode a single digit or adjacent two digits (if they are<=26).
in the base case when we have traversed whole string return 1(that would be one decode).
so dp[i]=dp[i+1]
if(two digit number <26 is formed) dp[i]+=dp[i+2].
He asked if there is any corner cases missing.I did'nt got any but then he said run for "2020" then i got it and said there can not be a decode for "0" alone we will have to match '0' with another digit so that the two digit number is <=26.i was asked to correct code for the same.
This was all then he said if i have any questions.
I asked about what u guys expect a fresher to do.He said u should have basic dsa oops and dbms knowledge .Based on that we train you about the algorithms for marketing we already have developed.
I asked do we need to have a knowledge of od,ml and webdev as these will be coming in upcoming semesters he said that dsa,oops and dbms are enough to work at gs.

other_comments:
My advice to anyone reading this would be just be interactive.If there are any doubt ask them.The interviewer will also sometimes give u a hint but you have to approach.Anything that you are thinking be it a brute solution.Just tell the interviewer and communicate as much as possible.
Also always be prepared with general questions like introduction(upto 5 mins)
Why are u interested in this company
What are your skills,strengths,weaknesses  etc.

online_test.description:
OT was perfectly designed having all type of difficulty problems, I was able to solve 1 complete and 2 partial. 22 students were shortlisted including me.

Preparation:
Took part in all contests on codeforces for 6 months, solved around 50-60 problems ( 1200-1500 rating) on codeforces.
participated in 9-10 leetcode contests(weekly and bitweekly) in 2 months

Suggestions:
in OTs solving quickly and smartly matters rather than keep solving same question whole OT.
So to speed up, give as many contests as you can and don't worry about ratings.. focus on your speed and accuracy..
Learn CP topics such as Binary search, STL, sliding window, Segment trees, DP, graph algorithms (DFS,BFS,shortest path majorly)
you can refer cpalgorithms,geeksforgeeks website, codeforces,leetcode blogs, YT channels: Tushar Roy, Aditya verma, HealthyUG

During OT:
After reading problem, first think for most basic approach then think about efficient one depending on constraints.. if after 5 minutes you couldn't come up with most efficient then start coding basic approach and check howmany cases passed, meanwhile mostly you will get some hint/idea so make changes and again check number of cases.. if it passed 80% + cases then move to next problem, if not then read your code and try to find corner case for each part of code.. keep making changes and checking for 5-10 minutes if no improvement then leave it and move to next problem, repeat same process.. by this method you will solve 3 problems partially and you will have some 20-30 minutes left so give that to problem for which you are most confident that you can solve it completely so make it 100% and chances of getting shortlisted are very high!!
All the best!!
questions:
It was a permutations based question, given 3 strings find lexicographically smallest string such that all 3 strings can be found as substring in your answer string.
input :  abc cde bcd
output : abcde

approach: for all 6 permutations of 3 strings, concatenate common characters in prefix and suffix of middle string and store 6 results, print lexicographically smallest out of them..

It was a tough category question based on sliding window, given 2 arrays that can have repeating elements, you need to find number of subarrays in one array such that that subarray must contain at least all elements of second array... ( subarray can have extra elements also)
example :
arr1 = [ 1 , 1, 2, 3, 3]
arr2 = [ 1 , 2, 3]
ans: 4
exp: [1,1,2,3] [1,1,2,3,3] [1,2,3] [1,2,3,3]

Approach:
keep track of elements counter using map and move your sliding window accordingly while keep adding number of indices in your answer..

Resource to learn Sliding window : https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PL_z_8CaSLPWeM8BDJmIYDaoQ5zuwyxnfj

It was hard level question of DP. given a 2d binary matrix where 0 means obstacle and 1 means open path, find number of unique paths from (0,0) to (m,n), also print lexicographically largest path. (we are allowed to move in 3 directions down,right and diagonally right-down)
Approach:
at any index (i,j) number of distinct path will be DP[i][j] then DP[i][j]=DP[i+1][j]+DP[i][j+1]+DP[i+1][j+1] and base case will be DP[m-1][n-1]=1
and DP[0][0] will be count of number of unique paths which is our answer.. now to find lexicographically largest path, we can maintain one 2d vector of pair , vector<vector<pair<int,int>>>next; for index (i,j) next[i][j] will store index of your next cell in answer path. so while filling pur DP array, we will check if (i+1,j+1) was '1' then we will make next[i][j]={i+1,j+1} else if (i+1,j) was '1' then next[i][j]={i+1,j} else next[i][j]={i,j+1} and we can run while loop starting from i=0,j=0 ; print (i,j) pair p=next[i][j]; i=p.first,j=p.second; while(i!=m-1 || j!=n-1)

almost same question : https://www.interviewbit.com/problems/unique-paths-in-a-grid/

interview_rounds.title: round 30
interview_rounds.description:
It started with my introduction and then quickly jumped to a coding question.
it was based on binary search + observation. even after solving so many observational problems on CF i found it very difficult to solve this one. I took around 30 minutes and 1 hint from interviewer. I was asked to write pseudo code for it and he was satisfied with it.

Problem desc.:
Given an array which was sorted initially but then some odd index elements were swapped with each other.. now perform search for a key in new array... (you will be given new array only)
eg. 1 8 5 2 10 30 24 15 , key = 12 output : NO
expected TC : logn

approach: we have to run binary search , depending on mid is odd or even we will check if mid  or mid-1 (whichever is odd) is at its original position or not, ( if we want to check if i is at correct pos or not then check a[i-1]<a[i] && a[i]<a[i+1] ) if it is then check for left or right segment depending on a[mid]<k or >k and if not correct pos then our problem is changed to find correct position for that element and check if our key is present at that position or not... ( this sounds complex and trust me it was but I managed to explain him with one example and he was satisfied ) ( major hint was that an element will be swapped at most once..  now it might be more clear..)

after that he asked other interviewer to ask further ( there were 2 interviewers)
he started with a DSA problem in which I need to design some data structures according to given requirement.. ( i was allowed to use any data structure already available also). He didn't want me to code but he just wanted to check my thinking process and how I am improvising according to new requirements.. after I told how I will implement they both asked me different questions regarding TC and SC of data structures I used, they were satisfied with the answer. ( it took around 25 minutes for this one)

Problem:
Given an industry of goods packaging. there are N boxes available with different package sizes each of them having M slots at different distances from source.. there will be an incoming parcel with a size, allot it in that size box if available otherwise allot to nearest bigger available size box.. if there are many slots available for a size then allot one nearest to source.... also there will be exit where we will remove package out of the box ( we will search using barcode)...

Approach:
I used unordered_map<pair<int,set<int>>> to store available slots for each size and other same map to store occupied ones but with barcode as key.. also one set of sizes to find nearest bigger available size box..TC for inserting will be O(logN*logM) (logN to find nearest bigger size box and logM to find slot in it) and removing will be O(logN*logM) again same explanation .. SC will be O(N*M).. they asked me doubts regarding how unordered_map works and how set is implemented how insertion and deletion takes only logN etc..

Next problem was based on OOP basically it was implementation of runtime polymorphism ( method overriding) i did it using abstract class first then he asked to do without it so i did using normal classes he asked doubts regarding runtime polymorphism and different access modifiers in java... ( it took 5-10 minutes for this question)

Last problem was from DBMS.. he asked me to create an employee database including different tables and then asked me to run some SQL query on it.. he was satisfied so didn't ask much.. ( it took around 10 minutes)

Total time was around 1 hour 30 minutes

after 10 minutes of round completion, I received message that next round was scheduled in 15 minutes..

8 students were shortlisted for round 2

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description:
Again there were 2 interviewers,
one of them started asking SQL queries.. he asked 4-5 queries and I answered all of them... ( all were basic queries related to aggregate functions, group by, order by)
He asked one flight scheduling problem .. i told to use priority_queue or set data structure... he didn't ask me to code...it was easy level problem..
then he asked me if i know edit distance , i replied affirmatively and explained meaning.. he asked me to write code for it.. it is a standard DP question so I wrote code in 2-3 minutes..

then he asked other interviewer to ask problems..
He started with problem based on hashing, I told the approach which was failing on some cases he gave me hint then i modified it but as I took lot of time already he didn't ask me to code that one..

Problem desc:
Given a string consisting of a,b,c find number of substrings having equal number of a,b,c in it...
eg abcabc
ans: 5
exp: abc,bca,cab,abc,abcabc
problem link:
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/substring-equal-number-0-1-2/

Next problem was given an chemical compound formula find frequency of each element in it... ( for ease of problem we have to assume all elements are single character). I gave recursive solution running in O(n) n=length of string where I used stack to find matching parenthesis..
I was asked to code it and I did it successfully..

problem Desc:
Ex: h2(m2(fg3)4c5)8
Output: h - 2, m- 16, f-32, g-96, c-40

Approach:
Start from j=n-1, do j-- and when you encounter ')' solve for i=match[j] to j where match[j] is index of matching '(' ... also send integer written after ')' in recursive call to multiply it... after making the call, do j=match[j]-1, (because we already solved match[j] to j)
To fill match array, keep one stack, start iterating string from beginning, when u get '(' push index and when u get ')' pop element from stack and do match[i]=s.top()...

Next problem was famous Burning tree problem..
I didn't solve problem before but I solved similar problem where fire is spreading just to children not parents.. so it took me 5-10 minutes to realise how problem will go, I explained my approach, I was asked to code for it and he was satisfied with the approach..

Problem link:
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/burn-the-binary-tree-starting-from-the-target-node/

Approach:
For each node find in it's left and right sub tree, function will return a pair where first element will represent if target node was found in that subtree and second element will represent a number depending on first value.. ( if first value is 1 (i.e. we found target node in that subtree) then second element will store depth of target node from root and if first value is 0 then second element will store depth of deepest node in that subtree from root... ) so every time we will update tot_time variable as .. if(p.first==1 || q.first==1)tot_time=max(tot_time,r.second+p.second-1)  ( basically it is nothing but depth of target node + depth of deepest node in opposite subtree - 1( root counted twice))  (here p is answer returned by left subtree, q is answer returned by right subtree)
and while returning, if(p.first==1 || q.first==1) return {1,(p.first==1)?p.second:q.second} else return {0,max(p.second,q.second)}..
It took me 10-15 minutes to explain my approach and code ...

Interview ended with this he asked if I had any questions for him then I asked about his experience at DE shaw ..

Total time for this was 1 hour 50 minutes

This was last round for all,
Next day results were announced, 3 students got intern ... my name was not in the list, though I felt it was a great experience for me so I shared with you guys : )

During Interview:
For coding problems,
First read whole problem in your words and keep telling what you are understanding from it line by line, after that think about corner cases and ask if they will be present in given input or not, let's say if problem is about finding max product in array then you should ask if zeroes or negative elements are present in array... Such doubts make good impression because they intentionally hide such things...
Avoid asking constraints, sometimes it turns out as bad impression because it shows that you are narrowing down your thinking process by
getting idea of TC from before itself.. so just try to optimise your TC slowly... once you are done if interviewer is not satisfied you will be asked to reduce TC/SC accordingly so don't worry about that much...
first try to think for most basic ( brute approach) tell it to interviewer, its TC and SC.. if he agrees with logic move to optimisation of given approach.. keep talking whatever you are thinking that will help you if you misunderstood some part of problem so interviewer can correct you there only... if you are asked ,"Are you sure that will work?" then think for 3-5 minutes for corner case if you don't find any then tell yes, after that if you are given any corner case then make changes according to that... once logic is finalised but you are unable to optimise it feel free to ask for hint, now carefully listen, depending on the hint you have to get idea if you need to optimise your brute approach or need to think something else, for example if hint is like , "While you are finding this or that..."  "While you are performing that operation..." etc statements then you need to optimise you brute approach otherwise think new approach using hint from scratch..
At least tell 2 approaches (Brute and one efficient) more than 2 are great!! but make sure to tell TC,SC everytime even if not asked..
Make sure you are not stuck in a loop ( repeating same and same approach again even after getting the hint )
When you are asked to code, while coding try to explain each and every line while you are writing itself specially for DP/recursion based solution what are you returning when and what will be base case, how you are managing recursive calls etc , if possible after writing the code, perform a dry run on sample or your own made test case that will make a good impression..

After results:
Even if you didn't get selected for any particular round or final shortlisting don't lose hope.. it is tough situation but actually intern drive is all about facing that situation only.. it's less about getting intern and more about handling failure according to me...talk to your friends,parents.. don't think what they will think about you, just go for it... There's always one company waiting to hire you, you never know which one is chosen for you otherwise you will skip all previous attempts and just go for that one but yeah it's all part of the plan so don't lose hope until you reach that chance... so try to stay happy and never ever let your confidence level down, that's the key... stay confident in your nth interview as it is your first one!!! as L ( death note) said , "I have lost the battle, but I am not going to lose the war!" : ) All the best!!

Illusive OR operation
You are given an array A (1-based) of size N and there is an operation called Illusive OR operation.
In one operation. choose two distinct indices i and j where1<= i,j <= N then after the operation set A[i] =A[i] | A[j].
You are given Q queries which are of two types:
. Type 1: You are given two integers pos and val and you have to set A[pos] = val.
. Type 2 You are given two integers L and R and you have to tell the maximum possible value of an array element between positions L and R,
you can get after doing Illusive OR operation any number of times with elements present between positions L and R.

Input:
3
1 3 5
3
2 1 3
1 2 4
2 1 2

Output:
7
5

online_test.description:

2. Coding skills :-  in this section there were problems related to data structures, all of them were from BST and AVL trees. There were mainly two main questions having code of around 50 lines and rest of the questions were based on these main questions.

job_description:
Job role : Intern Trainee
Apply widely agreed software engineering principles and methodologies to design, develop, test and maintain applications and services to achieve the stated business and technology goals within required budgets and timelines.

online_test.description:
Total Duration: 1 hour 30 minutes

Section 1: Coding
The test had two coding questions, both requiring strong problem-solving skills:

First Question: This was a medium to hard difficulty level problem that could be approached using a greedy strategy. It required solid problem-solving skills and a clear approach to reach the optimal solution.

Second Question: This was a harder problem that involved graph-based techniques and required breaking the problem into manageable subproblems to solve efficiently.

I managed to finish both questions just in time by applying a methodical approach and focusing on the most efficient solution.

Section 2: Behavioral Assessment
After the coding questions, the test shifted to a behavioral part, which was a bit different but equally important. This section was designed to assess how well you align with Amazon's leadership principles. Unlike the coding part, there was no strict time limit for this section, but it was suggested to complete it in about 20-30 minutes. I ended up taking about 1 hour to carefully go through the scenarios and answer the questions.

How it worked: We had to answer multiple-choice questions based on different scenarios. These scenarios were presented through an inbox interface, where you'd receive emails describing various situations. Based on these, we had to pick the best possible response, which would reflect how we would handle situations in a real-world professional environment, keeping Amazon's core values in mind.

This section helped assess how well candidates could apply leadership principles in practical situations, and it was a great way to wrap up the test.

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description:
Total Duration: 1 hour

Question 1: Number of Square Matrices with All 1's
The interviewer first asked me to code a brute-force solution for the problem. After implementing that, I optimized my solution and successfully coded a more efficient approach. I dry-ran 2-3 test cases myself to ensure the logic was working correctly.

You can find the problem details here:
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/dsa/number-of-square-matrices-with-all-1s/

Question 2: Subarrays with K Different Integers
For this question, I was not asked to code it. Instead, the interviewer asked me to explain my approach. I began by explaining a brute-force solution, then followed up with the optimized approach. I discussed how the sliding window technique could be used to efficiently solve the problem and improve time complexity..

You can find the problem details here:
https://leetcode.com/problems/subarrays-with-k-different-integers/

other_comments: One important takeaway from the interview: If you're confident in your approach, the interviewer might try to test that confidence by asking if you're sure your solution is correct. Don't let that trip you up. Stand by your approach and explain why you're confident in it. Interviews might try to see how you handle pressure and whether you can defend your solution logically.

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description:
There were two interviewers in this round.​
We started with a quick basic introduction, and then they jumped into the questions.

➤ DSA Question:
Q: Find the first missing positive number in an unsorted array.​
this was standard question, I walked them through a few different approaches...

➤Some Basics OOPs concepts then

➤ System Design using OOPs:

Classes to consider: Restaurant, Customer, DeliveryAgent
●​ Created an Order class to act as the bridge between Customer, Restaurant, and DeliveryAgent​

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description:
Again, there were two interviewers, and this time they skipped the intro and went straight into coding problems.

➤ Problem 1: Tax Collection Cutoff
Q:​ Given an array of salaries, the government wants to fix a cutoff C.
●​ Salaries < C → taxed 10%​
●​ Salaries ≥ C → taxed 50%​
We need to find the maximum value of C such that the total tax collected is at least B.

I used a binary search approach:
●​ For each mid value, I traversed the array to calculate the total tax.​
●​ If tax ≥ B, we can try a higher C.​

➤ Follow-up 1: Multiple Queries
Now I had to calculate C for multiple B values.
My initial approach was to use the same binary search function for each B — but that's O(n * log n) per query.
Then I optimized:
●​ Sorted the salary array once.​
●​ Built a prefix sum.​
●​ For each query, used binary search to calculate tax faster.​
Time reduced to O(n log n) from O(n2 log n)​

➤ Follow-up 2: Salary Updates
Now they made it trickier — salaries can be updated at any time, and we still have to calculate C efficiently.
I proposed using a Segment Tree to:
●​ Handle range tax queries.​
●​ Support point updates when a salary changes.​
It's hard to implement fully on the spot, but I explained the structure with pen and paper.

➤ Follow-up 3: Insert/Delete Salaries Dynamically
This was the toughest part. Now we had to support:
●​ Inserting new salaries​
●​ Deleting old ones​
...and still efficiently calculate C.​
I couldn't come up with a complete solution, but I discussed ideas like using AVL trees for balancing and why combining it with segment trees would be tricky.

➤ Again a designing question (Pointer Concepts):
After that, the second interviewer asked me about pointer-related problems in C++.
I mentioned:
●​ Wild pointers (e.g., uninitialized pointers)​
●​ Dangling pointers (when memory is freed but the pointer still exists)​
●​ Memory leaks​
He then asked me to implement a kind of smart pointer / wrapper class that prevents such problems.
I created a wrapper that:
●​ Initialized constructor with null ptr, and required parameterized constructor.​
●​ Maintains a static unordered_map to keep track of how many pointers are referencing a given heap address.​
●​ Frees memory in the destructor only when no other pointer is pointing to that address.

interview_rounds.title: Round 3
interview_rounds.description:
➤ Problem: Most Frequent Element in O(1)
Q:​ Design a data structure that supports:
●​ push(x) : Insert element​
●​ mfu() : Remove and return the most frequent element, and if tie → return most recently pushed​
Example:
●​ push(1) → [1]
●​ push(1) → [1, 1]
●​ push(2) → [1, 1, 2]
●​ push(3) → [1, 1, 2, 3]
●​ push(2) → [1, 1, 2, 3, 2]
●​
●​ mfu() → 2 → [1, 1, 2, 3]
●​ mfu() → 1 → [1, 2, 3]
●​ mfu() → 3 → [1, 2]
●​ mfu() → 2 → [1]
And all operations had to be O(1).

➤ My Approach:
●​ Used two maps:​
○​ element → frequency​
○​ frequency → stack of elements to track recency​
●​ A maxFreq variable to keep track of the current highest frequency.​

➤ They asked me about virtual functions and how they work.

other_comments:
● In the first round, they asked about my MCA curriculum and the subjects covered. Since we hadn't studied DBMS, they skipped DBMS questions (though they asked them to BTech students).
●​ No project-related questions were asked.​

questions: We were given two arrays — planCost (base plans) and featureCost (additional features). A user can select one plan and one feature, and the total cost is their sum. However, some plan-feature pairs were incompatible and could not be chosen. The task was to find the maximum possible subscription cost among all valid combinations. The main idea was to try the largest combinations while skipping the forbidden pairs. This problem mainly tested greedy thinking, sorting, and use of hash sets to store invalid pairs.

We were given an array of product prices. In one operation we can increase one product price by p and decrease another by p (p ≤ k). The goal was to perform the minimum number of operations so that the difference between the highest and lowest prices becomes strictly less than d. This problem required observing that every operation reduces the range between max and min prices, so the solution is based on greedy range reduction and simple mathematical calculation.

online_test.description:
The Amazon Online Test had multiple sections including code debugging, aptitude, coding, behavioural questions, and a feedback section. One important thing to remember is that the feedback must be submitted at the end; otherwise the test will not be considered for evaluation.

The debugging section was quite easy and mostly involved identifying small logical or syntax errors in the code. If you read the question carefully once, each problem can be solved very quickly.

The aptitude section was of medium level and mainly based on basic mathematics concepts up to class 10th. A quick revision of common aptitude topics a day or two before the test should be enough. Practicing a few questions from sites like IndiaBix can help.

In the coding section there were two questions where you had to complete the given function. The questions are usually standard DSA problems, and Amazon often asks tree-related questions, so practicing common data structure problems is helpful.

The behavioural section had many questions based on your work style and decision making. These questions are often connected to your previous answers, so it's important to stay consistent. It also helps to go through Amazon's Leadership Principles before attempting the test.

online_test.description:
The OT was conducted offline in the CCPD seminar hall, invigilated by company representatives, and it had two sections. The first was a 40-minute Behavioral test consisting of 57 questions divided into three sections of 19 each. Each situation had five options that had to be ranked based on what you resonate the most with. Some questions also focused on self-awareness, such as identifying one's strengths, with options like "I can easily identify" or "I struggle identifying."
The second section was a 35-minute coding test on Codility, conducted in Python only, with a basic template provided. It had three easy questions:
(1) Given two scores, return their difference
(2) Check if a number is odd

The problems were straightforward and didn't require any prior Python experience.

interview_rounds.title: round 10
interview_rounds.description:
We were asked to come to CCPD by 9 but they started calling in people to interview from around 10. I got called in around 11ish and my interviewer was the director of R&D in AI. He started by giving his introduction and then asked for mine. I had an ML project in my resume and his focus was entirely on that. He asked me about basic pre-processing techniques that I would apply in different situations and other basic ML concepts that you would generally know if you've done a project on it. He also asked me if I had experience working with RAG or MCP's, but I wasn't that familiar with them. Its okay to not know something as long as you can convey that you have the drive to learn if the situation arises. He then asked me about my interests and I was more into Cloud at the time and that's what I said. Luckily there was a team focused on AWS there and he sent me to that team for my next round.
Usually there are only 2 rounds technical focused and one HR round but I had 4 rounds as this first round didn't quite test my technicals in the domain I was interested in.

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description:
I was sent to one of the core AWS teams. I was asked how I implemented AWS on my project. What's load balancing why is it used, whats horizontal-vertical scaling and what recent developments were integrated into AWS. Then they shifted onto asking OOPS concepts like the 4 pillars, how can you access methods of a class without creating an object, what's the core differences between C++ and Java. He asked me another question based on constructors.
I was then asked 2 basic C++ questions, one was write any sorting algorithm and explain, the other one was how would you check if a string/number is a palindrome. Then I was asked about how API's work, what are REST API's and how are they used in my project. A couple more questions on AWS and then I was asked why I had interest in cloud and have I done any certifications so far.

interview_rounds.title: Round 3
interview_rounds.description: Here again the interviewer's focus was on Cloud concepts and he asked me general questions on AWS. Basic things you would need to know to setup an AWS EC2 Instance, what are security groups, how did you use Lambda functions and why cloudwatch. I was then asked what were my strengths and weaknesses, why cloud again and what do I plan to do in the future with respect to cloud.

interview_rounds.title: Round 4
interview_rounds.description:
Basic questions like where are you from, future plans, did I do any AWS Certifications yet, why cloud (3rd time :sob).
I asked a couple follow up questions at the end and this round ended in max 30mins.

other_comments: Be familiar with the work the company does, their recent developments/integrations in their products etc... Knowing these help a lot in asking follow up questions to the interviewers at the end, it shows your genuine interest in the company and the effort that you put in researching beforehand. Also make sure you know everything in your resume in and out, if you're unsure if you can answer something that's on your resume, REMOVE IT. It doesn't need to look like there's a lot of skills you have to offer, rather show that you're perfect in whatever you've mentioned on it. Side note make sure you leave a really good impression to the interviewers in the first round itself, later it gets very hard. Show that you stand out

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description:
Round 1 interview lasted around 40–45 minutes and was conducted by a two-member panel.

It began with a brief self-introduction while they reviewed my resume, followed by a short discussion of my projects with no deep project-specific questions.

The first technical task was to write pseudocode for checking a palindrome, but with a twist — if the string was a palindrome, the function had to return both true and its mirror substring (for example, "abccba" -> true, abc). I implemented it using two functions since I wasn't sure how to return multiple values from one function, though the interviewer wanted it done within a single function.

After that, I was asked about OOP concepts like encapsulation and inheritance, their uses, and benefits.

Next came a DSA question on finding the maximum sum subarray with an added condition that the elements of the subarray must be in increasing order, for which I wrote the pseudocode.

This was followed by questions on polymorphism (types and importance) and finding the height of a binary tree.

They also asked how we distributed work in our DBMS project, what challenges we faced, and how we tackled them.

The interview ended with me asking, "How is a general day for an intern at AVEVA?"

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description:
After about 7-10 minutes of completing Round 1, I was immediately called for Round 2, which had a single-member panel and lasted for about 30-35 minutes.

The round began with the interviewer introducing himself, after which he asked me to give my introduction and explain my projects in detail.

He then moved on to a technical question: asking me to explain Depth First Search (DFS) and write the pseudocode for it.

After that, the interview shifted to behavioral and situational questions. He asked about my major strength and weakness, followed by two situation-based scenarios:

If a project plan is made but midway you realize there's a mistake, how would you ensure the project still meets the deadline?

What would you do if you found out that your teammates weren't contributing to their part of the project and were delaying it?

Finally, he asked if I had any questions for him, to which I asked, "What has made you work at AVEVA for 11 years, and how is the workload for interns?"

interview_rounds.title: Round 2
interview_rounds.description:
After about 5 minutes of finishing Round 2, I was called for Round 3, which was an HR round conducted by the main HR and lasted for around 20 minutes.

The HR began by asking where I am based out of, followed by questions about my short-term and long-term goals related to both my career and life.

He then asked about my strengths and requested that I share a situation where I demonstrated them.

Next, we had a discussion on whether I would choose a company based on its CTC or other factors.

We also discussed my plans for higher studies, my preferred work location, and the work AVEVA does as a company.

Towards the end, he asked if I had any questions, and I asked, "What all tech stack should I know before joining AVEVA?"

It was a very chill and friendly interview, and the HR concluded by asking me to have lunch and come back later for the final selection results.

other_comments:
Always show genuine interest in the company and its work. While solving questions, discuss your approach aloud to keep the interviewer engaged and never make them sit idle waiting for your response. Maintain a positive attitude throughout the interview; keep a smile on your face, but stay composed even if you feel nervous.

Always clarify the question before answering and let the interviewer complete their question before you start speaking. If you don't know the answer to something, honestly admit it instead of trying to guess.

Finally, remember that your communication skills, confidence, and overall presentation play a major role in creating a strong impression, often as important as your technical knowledge.

online_test.description: The pattern for OT, was quite different from that of most companies. It consisted on MCQs, with no negative marking. Each section was seperately time bound, so time remaining in any section cannot be transferred to other section. Time is an important factor as there are many MCQs; make sure you atleast attempt every question. Suggested order of solving the sections would be Contextual Communication, Computer Science Knowledge, Software Engineering Aptitude and Coding skills. Aptitude section is easy to score but has very tight time constraint, so manage time wisely. Try to keep coding skills for the last as it is usually tricky an requires more thinking. Try to solve all questions, do not leave anything empty.

online_test.description:
Online OT
4 DSA questions
questions: Linchpin Agents (The Syndicate)  Problem Statement: You are a cryptic analyst known only as "Cipher," tasked with mapping the command structure of a clandestine global network called "The Syndicate." The syndicate is comprised of agents who communicate through secure, encrypted channels.  Your analysis suggests that the network isn't hierarchical. Instead, its resilience relies on a decentralized web of connections. However, you hypothesize that there are certain "Linchpin Agents" who, by the very structure of the network, are indispensable for information flow.  A Linchpin Agent is not defined by their number of connections, but by their critical position as a communication bridge. An agent X is a linchpin if there exist at least two other agents, A and B, for whom every possible communication path from A to B absolutely requires passing through agent X.  If a linchpin agent is compromised, all communication between those specific agents A and B would be completely severed, no matter how many alternative routes exist elsewhere in the network. Your mission is to identify all such linchpin agents to understand the network's true vulnerabilities.

Marble Elimination Game   Problem Statement: You're playing a modified version of a strategic color-elimination game.  On a narrow path, there's a single line of marbles, each painted with one of five colors:  Red (R), Yellow (Y), Blue (B), Green (G), or White (W)  You also hold a few marbles in your hand. Your mission is to completely eliminate the marbles from the path using as few moves as possible.  Game Rules: - On your turn, you may insert exactly one marble from your hand anywhere along the path — either between two existing marbles or at either end. - If, after inserting a marble, a group of three or more consecutive marbles of the same color forms, that group will burst and disappear. - This bursting can cascade: if removing one group leads to another group of three or more of the same color, those also burst — and this continues until no such groups remain. - Your objective is to clear the entire path of marbles. - If you run out of marbles in your hand and the path still has marbles, the mission fails.  Task: Write a function to determine the minimum number of marbles you need to insert from your hand to clear the entire path. If it's not possible to clear the path using the given marbles in hand, return -1.

WWE: Money in the Bank  Problem Statement: The WWE's Money in the Bank match is underway, and the audience is electrified as superstars battle it out to climb the ladder and grab the coveted briefcase. The ladder itself, however, is a special one—it has a unique structure that needs to be analyzed.  You are given an array of n integers, representing the heights of ladder steps from the bottom to the top. Additionally, there are m match scenarios, where each scenario is defined by two integers, li and ri. These integers represent the starting and ending steps of a segment of the ladder that a superstar might climb during the match.  For each match scenario, you need to determine if the selected segment of the ladder is climbable. A segment is considered climbable if it follows the "ladder pattern." The "ladder pattern" is defined as follows:  1. The steps first rise or remain the same in height (non-decreasing). 2. Then, at some point, the steps descend or remain the same in height (non-increasing).  In other words, there must exist a step x (within the segment) such that: * The steps from li to x do not decrease in height, and * The steps from x to ri do not increase in height.  Even if the segment is entirely non-decreasing or entirely non-increasing, it is still considered climbable.  Your task is to analyze each match scenario and determine if the segment of the ladder satisfies the "ladder pattern." Are you ready to assist the WWE superstars in their quest for the Money in the Bank briefcase?

Graph Sequences   Problem Statement: You are given a positive integer N and a length N of string S consisting only of 0 and 1.  Consider a simple undirected graph G with N+1 vertices numbered 0, 1, 2, ..., N and the following edges:  - For each i=0,1,..., N−1 there is an undirected edge between vertices i and (i+1) mod N. - For each i=0,1,..., N−1 there is an undirected edge between vertices *i* and *N* if and only if s_i = 1. - There are no other edges.  Furthermore, create a directed graph G' by assigning a direction to each edge of G. That is, for each undirected edge {u, v} in G, replace it with either a directed edge (u, v) from u to v or a directed edge (v, u) from v to u.  For each i=0,1,..., N−1, let **d_i be the in-degree of vertex i in G'.  Print the number, modulo 998244353, of distinct sequences (d_0, d_1,..., d_N) that can be obtained.

interview_rounds.title: Round 1
interview_rounds.description:
I had a single interview round, conducted online with two panelists. The interview covered technical, project-based, and HR aspects. As soon as I mentioned my projects during the introduction, they immediately asked me to elaborate on them. From there, the discussion moved to Object-Oriented Programming, where I explained the four pillars, serialization, error handling, and deadlocks. They further asked about the causes of deadlocks and the methods to prevent them.

The conversation then shifted to Operating Systems, where I was asked about different scheduling processes, their advantages and disadvantages, and also given a situation where I had to choose the most suitable scheduling method. They also asked me to explain various memory management techniques, starting from continuous allocation to paging and segmentation, and finally the evolution towards virtual memory. I explained in detail the role and importance of virtual memory in modern systems.

For the coding part, I was asked to write a struct with two integers and a character, then I was asked to explain how many bytes it would consume and how memory allocation works with padding. In the HR segment, I was asked how I would ensure that users consume advertisement content within an application without closing it, and what types of advertisements I would suggest. My answers were well received, and the panelists seemed impressed. Towards the end, they invited me to ask questions, and we had a brief discussion about their products and how the company promotes sustainable development.

other_comments: Overall, it was a truly enriching experience — the panelists were approachable and encouraging, and the online test was well-structured and fair.
